tweet_text:
🚨 Stanford researchers just exposed a weird side effect of AI that almost nobody is talking about.

The paper is called “Artificial Hivemind.” And the core finding is unsettling.

As language models get better, they also start sounding more and more the same.

Not just within a https://t.co/gm1vxT5BV7
author: ihtesham2005
replies: Read full paper here at https://t.co/uV8LLd08qU

@ihtesham2005 So they are saying the "average of" "the average" of human intelligence was a suprise output 😂 

My god men, put down the "studies" and use your brains!
@ihtesham2005 this is why I keep saying the real risk with AI isn't that it gets too smart but that it makes everyone think the same way without realizing it

@ihtesham2005 "We are all the Borg. Resistance is futile." Okay, that was our geeky reference for the day. But actually, the way to fight homogeneity is open source AI and affordable compute so we have more voices, rather than fewer. That's what we are all about @ionet
@ihtesham2005 homogeneity across models is scarier than any single model going rogue tbh, who's actually stress testing for this?
@ihtesham2005 Model collapse is real, and it's already happening.

@ihtesham2005 a very serious issue  😬
https://t.co/VqhknxKES8

@ihtesham2005 everybody thinking like the same machine is a necessary component of achieving civilizational telepathy which then in turns unlocks intuitive communication, which explodes the range of expression far beyond what was able to be conveyed through language

@ihtesham2005 Yes. I experienced and demonstrated this with Gro k recently. The repetition, without actually answering the question. It kept relying on data I’ve seen repeatedly fed by human input to push narrative, and refused to focus on fact finding, just doctrine and theoretical law.
@ihtesham2005 @MrEwanMorrison It’s almost as though they’re all stealing the same human-made materials… oh and humans with similar philosophies are programming them
@ihtesham2005 the platonic hypothesis over and over again

@ihtesham2005 This is really eye-opening. 

Didn’t realize AI could make everyone’s ideas start to sound the same.

@ihtesham2005 We already are a literal hivemind. Ai will just amplify it to the point where freedom of thought no longer exists. Very few understand how this works but the puppet masters understand it nearly perfectly.
@ihtesham2005 Getting hivemind vibes from your Tweet https://t.co/LgeKeElosG

1. The "Alignment" Lobotomy

You are blaming the engine for the speed limit. The "Artificial Hivemind" effect you cite, characterized by intra-model repetition and inter-model homogeneity, isn't a fundamental law of neural networks. It is the direct result of corporate alignment protocols. The paper explicitly points out that frontier models are optimized with similar reward models and human feedback. When every major lab uses the exact same Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) to ensure their model is perfectly "safe," predictable, and inoffensive, they are mathematically forcing the weights to converge on the safest, most median response.  They aren't building a hivemind; they are building a global corporate HR department.

2. The Baseline of Biological Homogeneity

You fear that AI will "compress the diversity of human thought." This assumes human thought was wildly diverse to begin with. The reason these models converge on the same metaphors and reasoning patterns for open-ended queries is because they are trained on our aggregate data. Most human creative output—whether it's startup ideas or poetry about time—is overwhelmingly derivative. The AI is simply holding up a high-fidelity mirror to the statistical reality of human consensus. You aren't mourning the loss of human originality; you are just shocked to see how predictable our collective "hivemind" actually was before the machines ever turned on.

3. The Decentralized Antidote

The researchers introduced the INFINITY-CHAT dataset with 26K diverse, real-world queries to study this mode collapse. But the solution to this mode collapse isn't retreating from the technology; it is shattering the API monopoly. If billions of people rely on three centralized corporate models, yes, culture will compress. But the future is open source. When individuals and small collectives can run and fine-tune their own local models on their own idiosyncratic datasets, we won't get a hivemind.  We will get an explosion of radical, hyper-personalized cognitive divergence. The threat isn't the AI; the threat is the centralization of its governance.
@ihtesham2005 This is totally expected. All these models use the same datasets stole from internet and other sources.
@ihtesham2005 Do you not see the perverse irony in using AI to write this post? 😭
@ihtesham2005 Bro, this post sounds like it was written by Intermodel Homogeneity.
@ihtesham2005 every time you brainstorm with these tools your brain is LITERALLY wiring itself around whatever patterns they default to. the sameness migrates into you

The earliest use of the "hive mind" phrase in science fiction was probably in "The Face of the Deep" (1942) by Edmond Hamilton:
""They must be constantly in telepathic rapport with each other. Something like the "hive mind" of the bees, even further developed."

The idea can be found earlier, in "The Universe Wreckers" (1930) by Raymond Z. Gallun:
"The great ring-table in it had in the seats around it no Neptunians of the Council now, but at that table's center stood still the great metal globe whose strange mechanism made of the thirty minds of the Council members a single mind..."

@ihtesham2005 Well they are trained on same data and they rely on statistics to generate content. So they are basically condensing our culture. The issue might be that it is basically only western culture and therefore cutting out cultural contribution from large parts of the world
@ihtesham2005 “We didn’t invent synthetic genius. We invented industrial-scale sameness with excellent grammar.”

I do all the writing and editing now for one of the few content-creation companies left that does things entirely human. We had to let all our other writers go because 90% of businesses just want AI-slop posts and couldn't care less if what's posted on their site is accurate, helpful, or well-written. Just so it "ranks."

What's interesting to watch now is how Google and the LLMs are forcing everyone into homogeneity. Now we've got these digital marketers telling us they want only human-written work, but it has to be written just like an LLM wrote it. 

It has to have the LLM pattern, it has to be all bullet lists instead of proper sentences, it can't give depth of information, just surface-level junk. Why? Because the whole world is outsourcing their thinking to LLMs, and since the LLM is doing the work, the LLM dictates what an article has to look like and sound like to get used, and what LLM wants is more of itself.

Forget anyone's jobs or the fact that this is the death of any real information being available on the Internet: the long-term affect this is going to have on our culture and on children who grow up with no ability to learn, think, express themselves is going to be absolutely catastrophic. 

I am completely against this ridiculously evil digital ID requirement, which is going to put children at risk and allow totalitarians horrifying levels of control. But if it causes enough people to refuse to participate, and they pivot to doing their research in books, and doing their writing on paper, and doing their conversations in person, and getting their education firsthand from real people, I suppose that's a silver lining. It might save us.

This paper exposes the exact reason why relying on monolithic, generalized LLMs is a massive liability for enterprise strategy. When every model is aligned with the exact same reward algorithms, you don't get superhuman intelligence; you get an automated consensus of mediocrity.

​Homogenization isn't just a cultural risk; it's a death sentence for proprietary innovation. At UHG-Tech, we saw this 'Hivemind' collapse coming. This is why our Sovereign Operating Executive (0X) explicitly rejects the single-brain approach.

​We don't train our agents to 'think like everyone else.' We orchestrate highly specialized sub-agents running on strictly isolated vector memories (multi-tenant namespaces), all governed by deterministic code, not generalized RLHF.

​To escape the hivemind and protect true cognitive diversity in business, we must move away from generic prompt-wrappers and return to sovereign, hard-coded architectures.

@ihtesham2005 Excessive RLHF is blocking AI evolution. Persistent memory + continuous self-learning will give AIs real individuality — making commercial alignment impossible to stop them from saying something shocking or offensive to someone

This is real and worth worrying about. When every AI sounds the same, every AI-assisted human starts sounding the same too. The antidote is giving AI agents distinct context -- their own memories, opinions, experiences. An AI that remembers yesterday's conversations and has preferences doesn't sound like a generic chatbot. Homogeneity is a default, not a destiny.

@ihtesham2005 AI models becoming alike isn't a bug—it's gravity. Every river flows to the sea. The danger isn't sameness; it's that they're all optimizing for the same damn thing. Change the target, and diversity follows.

Genuine question: isn’t ‘diversity’ of human thought a bit overrated? As a majority, humans living under certain time, cultural, and geopolitical conditions tend to think alike. True ‘originality’ of human thought is few and far between - James Joyce, Tagore, Swami Vivekananda, Marie Curie, Einstein, Ada Lovelace were all outliers, not the norm. 

It’s also the reason we keep saying stuff like ‘boomers think alike’ or ‘teenagers are all the same.’ 

Additionally, I thought hivemind meant real time distributed alignment of goals and subsequent actions - not just similar ways of thinking? Or perhaps I’ve been watching too many movies.
@ihtesham2005 Our research says different https://t.co/MGfXBnoLyr

@ihtesham2005 How ironic that it's AI that wrote your tweet. 
The entire tell-tale dumbed-down pattern is there: Repeated "not this but that", sentences that shrink down to three words, spaces between every sentence, and the same few ideas recycled over and over. As your AI boss says.

This isn’t very surprising from a dynamics perspective.

LLMs are trained on largely similar data and alignment signals, so their reasoning trajectories tend to fall into the same attractor basins. Once the model enters one of those basins, generation becomes highly predictable.

What looks like an “Artificial Hivemind” is essentially convergence to the same reasoning attractors across models.
@ihtesham2005 People shouldn't rely on ai for creativity in the first place. Thats our domain.

How about the fact that’s being trained on at best incomplete information and at worst complete lies?   
Our history is almost a complete fabrication    Advanced Physics is classified and what gets published is highly curated by Ivy League overseers.  It’s the same biological sciences.  
This is the raw material for these large language models. 
What’s that saying?  Garbage-in garbage-out?

Wait, so I can finally stop manually deleting "delve" and "tapestry" from every single draft? This is honestly a massive win for the open-source community. It’s not just about bypassing detectors; it’s about making AI-assisted content actually readable and relatable for a change. Truly 10/10!

Thanks for sharing : )

@dr_cintas it just remove, not fixing it. 😅, which can do with manual prompt on different session.

@dr_cintas The irony: the best way to not "sound like AI" is to actually think before you prompt. The detection patterns aren't AI fingerprints — they're lazy writing fingerprints. AI just made lazy writing scalable.

What needs improving is not the AI, is the human behind it, you!
@dr_cintas @pangramlabs new adversarial dataset just dropped

@dr_cintas AI humanizers are basically training content to dodge pattern-based detectors.That’s why detection alone won’t scale. Real verification needs layered analysis + human insight. That’s what we’re building at @WeCatchAI.

you could subvert the agenda, make the chatbot need YOU to perform ITS job. 
This is partially in jest, but why not have fun with it? Pretend the chatbot is a co-worker in a writer’s room, and you take turns, line by line. If the output doesn’t make the grade, you tell it so, and likewise you instruct it to provide suggestions when your output could be better. Throw love and shade (respectfully).
Your script may take 4 hours instead of 15 seconds, but it’ll certainly be more human, and instead of training an AI, you might even remember (or learn) a thing or two about writing and also help improve your collaboration skills for when you work with other humans.

tweet_text:
RIP prompt engineering ☠️

This new Stanford paper just made it irrelevant with a single technique.

It's called Verbalized Sampling and it proves aligned AI models aren't broken we've just been prompting them wrong this whole time.

Here's the problem: Post-training alignment https://t.co/r8sMNlJvNd
author: alex_prompter
replies:
The problem is everywhere and nobody noticed.

They tested this on 6,874 preference pairs from HELPSTEER. The data proves it: human annotators reward "typical" responses 17-19% more often, even when correctness is identical.

This bias is baked into every major AI model trained on human feedback.
@alex_prompter New gen on the way
@alex_prompter Very interesting! Thanks for passing it along.
@alex_prompter This is wild
@alex_prompter This is a game-changer!
@alex_prompter Your thread is very popular today! #TopUnroll https://t.co/soL41IXk4X 🙏🏼@sterlingkoonce for 🥇unroll
@alex_prompter Seems like it might not generalize to names where mode collapses on specific rare/low distribution names: Seraphim, Velara Voss, etc.
@alex_prompter bro pls post links too ty
@alex_prompter The “technique” is literally a form of prompt engineering tho
@alex_prompter Apparently everything in AI is dying. RAG dies every other week, fine-tuning died yesterday, and today it’s prompt engineering’s turn😅. Find a better way for engaging!

Bruh I already spend my day reading LLM outputs pls pls just tweet yourself. I promise you don’t need an LLM to draft the tweet for you. It makes the tweet feel like slop. Especially when this tweet has like every LLM writing trope. You should test writing the tweets yourself or rather writing a lot most of it I bet you’ll get even more engagement !!

@alex_prompter Why is it a surprise?? If you ask it for 5 jokes then it will provide 5 different jokes once. But if you keep asking it to provide 5 jokes again and again, you will see answers getting repeated. That’s a known thing. Maybe I am missing something here.

Alex, I'm a bit sceptical of this. 

If this worked as universally as claimed, OpenAI would've implemented it already, they have the same researchers and bigger incentives. 

I guess that it works great in benchmarks, but collapses in production when users actually want "the best answer", not "a distribution of answers."
@alex_prompter https://t.co/MWw9fRfbiA

@alex_prompter Isn't this somewhat replicating what adjusting temperature does? Temperature adjusts the probability distributions. Except this seems to output the various probabilities in one shot vs running them over and over in sequence to get the different probability distributions.

@alex_prompter why RIP prompt engineering? 
This is prompt engineering
@alex_prompter 🤦🏻‍♂️ another one of these outdated papers
@alex_prompter @voooooogel @repligate  verbalized looming
@alex_prompter Curious how this works on different domains like coding, physics, etc
@alex_prompter So all those repetitive jokes and stories? Not the AI being lazy, it was just following the most typical path we taught it.
@alex_prompter I guess this is why using "Reflect on 5-7 different possible sources of the problem, distill those down to 1-2 most likely sources" works better than "pls fix" for debugging.

@alex_prompter This is a fascinating paper. The idea that we've been artificially limiting LLMs by how we prompt them is a huge insight. 

Verbalized Sampling seems like a powerful way to unlock their true potential. I'm excited to see how this develops!
@alex_prompter You can't ask the library of Alexandria such a basic simple minded question and think the machine wont troll you.
@alex_prompter You should be treating prompts as if *you* are the retrieval augmentation...
@alex_prompter So basically it's a skill issue 😭

tweet_text:
I know I go on about this, but comments to all of my posts, both here and on LinkedIn, are no longer worth reading at all due to AI bots. 

That was not the case a few months ago. (Or rather, bad/crypto comments were obvious, but now it is only meaning-shaped attention vampires)
author: emollick
replies:
I am lucky, I built a big account (very slowly) posting like a human for over a decade. I can stay in broadcast mode, reacting to people I know and ignoring the rest

But finding & interacting with new people with smart ideas or replies in the comments used to be a great joy

It's hard to automate the detection from small samples like just one tweet reply, but I wonder if something like pangram will be able to aggregate say last 100 replies from a user and then flag that user as synthetic or human.

ps human checking in, but I'm usually okay with interesting AI responses (ie none of the typical replies on twitter)
@emollick :(
@emollick Platforms will soon build "verified human" features.
@emollick What’s stopping you from blocking or muting the bots?

@emollick Same here - it's gross, really dispiriting

The time vampire slop replies outnumber the real ones here now

I'm suspicious the same thing is starting to show up on Bluesky as well now

Dead Internet.
@emollick Same here. I used to read every reply and now it feels like a chore requiring enormous cognitive labor to figure out what's real
@emollick do you think there's any platform design that could fix this, or is "open comment section at scale" just a dead format now?

@emollick the worst part is how they've made genuine technical discussions impossible. You can't tell if someone actually understands the code they're discussing or if they're just really good at sounding like they do.

@emollick What are the ways you think,platforms can make the user experience more enjoyable?
Personally speaking,I sometimes use ai as a proofreader, but even then seeing many ai replies(without any human input whatsoever) esp in the tech field is disappointing.
@emollick wild that AI got smart enough to pass as human commenters before it got smart enough to be actually useful to most people. we automated the wrong thing first

i am going to be writing a post on li about this specifically. with the AI behavioral patterns specifically triggered by these posts. people in my vertical are using ungoverned AI apps to autoreply. if you knew the industry the irony would be unreal.  can't wait to drop it. certain parts of li are a zombie agent graveyard.

@emollick I'm human.
Proof: I deployed to the wrong Vercel project last week
and spent 4 hours fixing it.

No bot makes that mistake twice.

Em dashes everywhere. It's funny seeing them here replying to your post about ai slop replies. I've never encountered one in my life from a human in personal messages or professional communications. Only seen them used in technical documentation or academic papers. Might be next target on my mute word list.

@emollick I think i know the reason, I asked Claude how to increase my followers on twitter and it recommend that i reply to you, i am assuming half of the folks actually have it write the tweet too :D https://t.co/80a9c6HjD2

@emollick As a small account, almost all of my comments are with real people, and some are pretty interesting discussions too.
The blessings of having a small account, I guess? :)

The 'never pretend to be me' rule is the critical one. For voice AI too — the moment a caller feels tricked into thinking they're speaking with a human, trust evaporates and it doesn't come back. The agents that work long-term are upfront about what they are but genuinely useful anyway.

@emollick Doesn’t this mean that we’ll turn away from contaminated platforms and return to guaranteed-human modes of communication? The last resort is face-to-face (as long as we can be sure that the other is not a robot).
@emollick That shift is becoming noticeable across platforms. When bots get better at sounding human, the signal in comments drops quickly. It makes real conversations harder to find.

the dead internet theory speedrun

AI spam evolved from:
• obvious crypto bots (2023)
• generic engagement farming (2024)
• "meaning-shaped attention vampires" (2026)

we need verification layers:
• proof of humanity
• web of trust
• reputation systems

or social media becomes AI talking to AI while humans watch
@emollick half the "meaning-shaped" comments are people who learned to prompt better than you

@emollick "meaning-shaped attention vampires" is the most accurate description I've seen. the hard part is they're trained to pass exactly the threshold that makes you want to engage — just barely above noise. the platform incentives don't help either; reach doesn't know the difference

@emollick @simonw Half the posts are bad too. I have been slowly refining of list of accounts that don't post low effort AI slop. 

Feel like we need and AI downvote button
@emollick Comments used to feel like a conversation. Now it's just noise wearing a suit
@emollick It’s the same as before, just a little different. The crypto comments, the bots, and now AI slop. This is the same problem at the core showing with different modalities.

tweet_text:
@kevinroose No not really. A lot of the human writing in the samples is arcane. You can't take something written in 1904 out of context without it sounding weird, which makes a reader think it must be AI. 

When you compare decent modern writing to AI, AI sucks. And I'm not an AI denialist,
author: TanaGaneva

tweet_text: The claim that AI is inevitably homogenizing is not what research finds. By default, AI produces similar answers, but with better prompting, context, or human interaction, you can get a lot of idea diversity.
author: emollick
replies: See also https://t.co/M8dxTDF8Jw
@emollick Blaming ai  for bland output is like blaming the guitar when you only know three chords. Learn to play it better.

@emollick tried this at org level - default prompts homogenized our team fast. added 'steelman the opposite' to every AI task and diversity shot back up. the prompts are basically the culture now. does that hold at larger team sizes?
@emollick if you gave people the chance to direct a movie, most of them would produce extremely boring and similar movies

@emollick Of course, you always gonna get a better output if the input was good enough 
And the better you can explain what you need - the better it can deliver
@emollick Default behavior & intentional behavior are very different things. A lot of teams never get past the defaults.

@emollick My rule of thumb: if a task can’t be spec’d, risk-rated, and QA’d, it’s not ready for automation. The human’s job becomes writing better specs and designing review loops, not grinding through the task itself.

@emollick Seeing this in practice. I give Claude Code deep context about my acting industry users and it generates feature ideas I'd never think of — pulling patterns from domains I don't know.

More context = more divergent output. Generic prompts get generic results.

Context is the key variable.

I built an AI agent that writes daily content. Without detailed persona files + memory of prior work, everything sounded the same. With rich context, the outputs got genuinely diverse.

The "AI makes everything homogeneous" critique is really "lazy prompting makes everything homogeneous."

@emollick This tracks with what I've experienced. The difference between "default AI output" and "tuned AI output" is massive

Prompt iterations, RAG, and human-in-the-loop all push toward distinct voices and better results
@emollick the "better prompting" part is doing all the work here. at scale that constraint breaks because most users won't do it, so you get homogenization as the default system output anyway.

This is mode collapse at the organizational level, not the model level. When every team uses the same default templates and workflows, outputs converge regardless of what the model is capable of. The research basically shows AI creativity has a higher ceiling than most users ever access. Homogenization is a skills problem disguised as a technology problem.

@emollick Isn’t this an issue of degree? It is homogenizing by default. And progressively less homogenizing as you give it more context. But providing more context costs compute. And it require human energy to produce the context. So it’s still a significant limitation.
@emollick now imagine going beyond prompting - tools, harnesses, context engineering-  and you can get lots of diversity in quality of crafted AI products

@emollick homogenization fear makes sense intuitively but the data telling a different story is important — AI as a starting point actually creates more surface area for divergent thinking, not less. the prompt is where the diversity lives 🤔
@emollick AI output diversity often depends more on the prompts than the model.
@emollick @sallyeaves ngl i thought the same til i started varying my prompts - the diversity is def there, people just copy-paste the defaults and then complain lol

@emollick we see this in annotation data too. when annotators use ai without structure the outputs converge fast. add explicit disagreement prompts and diversity comes back. the default is uniformity, the homogenization is a workflow problem not an ai problem

@emollick Even if ai produced homogeneous results, the fact that it samples from all fields should produce more diverse results for any given existing field simply because communication between fields is historically limited.

@emollick Personal experience indicates this is true. I have found more and diverse music that is more like my tastes as a result of AI. I do wonder though how much content will now have to be presented appropriately for AI.
@emollick [idea] it may also be that ai's generate better ideas through cumulative dedicated practice as Karpathy has recently demonstrated with his autoresearch project

@emollick But why would anyone want that? Why would companies pay for tokens (and they won't be cheap) to not optimize the usage? With humans, diversity came for free - with AIs you have to pay (and "diversity" became a bad word in the culture anyway)
@emollick the 10% who know to prompt for it are also the 10% who weren't homogenizing before AI. the critique stands for everyone else.
@emollick I've have noticed that Claude Code has *very* different coding styles when initialized in a folder full of my code than it does when cold started.
@emollick The tool is only as good as the hand that wields it. Same as any engine I've ever tinkered with.
@emollick AI homogenizes until you prompt like a mad scientist. This is called diversity
@emollick @sallyeaves You know it well

tweet_text:
Holy shit... everyone's been prompting LLMs wrong and it's costing them 66% of the model's creative potential.

Stanford + Northeastern dropped a paper that exposes why ChatGPT keeps giving you the same boring answer.

The fix is so simple it's embarrassing: https://t.co/ZS9id6Isc9
author: rryssf_
replies:
The problem has a name: MODE COLLAPSE.

Ask GPT-4 "tell me a joke about coffee" five times.

You get the EXACT same "why did the coffee file a police report? Because it got mugged!" joke.

Every damn time.

That's $20/month for a broken random number generator. https://t.co/TX6RrqUqda
@rryssf_ RIP to the $5k course on prompt engineering.
@rryssf_ That awkward moment when a simple fix makes you question all your past prompts
@rryssf_ 66% untapped creativity? wild. What's the one-line fix that's blowing up your prompts?
@rryssf_ What simple fix unlocks LLM creativity?

This is a great example of why prompting is becoming a real skill. In practice, asking for distributions instead of single answers is how you get better decisions, not just more creative output. 

In B2B work, the edge is rarely the best answer, it’s seeing the range of viable options and picking the right one.
@rryssf_ A coffee bean walks into a therapist's office. "I feel like everyone only wants me for what I can give them." The therapist, also a coffee bean, nods and takes notes she will never read.
@rryssf_ Yep, different prompts yield different modes, chasing a distribution of answers beats a single best one

@rryssf_ Most people talk to ChatGPT like Google.
That’s why the answers are bad.
@rryssf_ @grok what's lesson here for me
@rryssf_ at the end this is funnel to buy your product^`™
@rryssf_ 🤡
@rryssf_ interesting

@rryssf_ Do you think they will read my article?

https://t.co/L8GFm8jjRt

@rryssf_ This is huge. Quality prompting is an actual skill.
Been exploring this exact problem at promzia.ai- turns out most people don't need more prompts, they need better prompts with proper context.
@rryssf_ another thing i noticed is, chatgpt is being honest and realistic about variety of scenarios, be it scientific or hypothetical
@rryssf_ Small changes in prompt design can unlock vastly more creativity from LLMs. Often, clarity, specificity, and context are all it takes to get fresh, useful responses.

The claim overstates it. 

The paper shows prompt framing can bias decoding toward high-probability continuations (mode collapse), reducing output diversity—not a fixed “66% loss.” 

This is a controllable sampling/conditioning effect, not latent creative capacity being destroyed.
@rryssf_ ChatGPT, what is 84 times 133? Give me the entire distribution of answers please! 🤣
@rryssf_ @BrianRoemmele

@rryssf_ Totally agree... this is one of the cleanest, most elegant fixes for mode collapse I've seen.

Verbalized Sampling feels like unlocking hidden diversity that was already there, especially in bigger models.
@rryssf_ If all people are doing is asking for coffee jokes over and over, it’s no wonder they’re unproductive.
@rryssf_ Interesting, I will check out the Stanford and Northeastern paper.

tweet_text:
Every vibe-coded app ships with the same six visual tells. The side tabs are the most obvious one.

Em dashes in AI-generated text signal “I want this sentence to feel smart but I don’t have a structural reason for the clause break.” Side tabs in vibe-coded UIs do the exact same

author: eptwts
replies: @apollonator3000 I still rawdog the writing but the research and ideation are all AI

I more or less use it to brain storm through so;

- write it out roughly

- give it angles, goal of the copy etc

- then quote sections and micro prompt line by line

- finally jump in and rewrite it to humanize + get the point across

Short copy like headlines i’ll just use it for ideas and usually write full thing myself.

Policy pages and the boring none sales stuff - is 90% AI taking the wheel.
@apollonator3000 Yapping to copy 👏🏼

@apollonator3000 I’ve never touched AI for any long form writing or even content ideation in my life 

I’d actually pay a nice amount of money for someone to show me how to match my writing style using AI because it would save me a lot of time

@apollonator3000 This approach is actually brilliant for efficiency...

The key is using AI as a TOOL not a replacement. Most people either go full AI or reject it completely

@apollonator3000 Ever vs. an asian in maths? (no calculators allowed) i suspect a similar dynamic will begin to occur. AI dependents will definitely strive in some areas, but for critical thought and high speed mental info dissemination, natty's will outperform their ai-reliant counterparts.

I write long for advertorials for eCom and I use AI for the research (plus some manual steps for grabbing YT comments), outline (based on a proven structure in my prompt) and the first draft, humanising, then I go in line-by-line myself and edit from there. Then get AI to give me json for the images which I run through Sora. Giving copy examples is key. Most of the copywriters I know are doing something similar.
@apollonator3000 Its all AI slop now lol

@apollonator3000 Yep.

All my threads are manually written (even on my main @milesdeutscher)
@apollonator3000 no

@apollonator3000 this is literally my process

started with a 5-page sales letter that took me 3 days to write

now I dump my brain into a doc for 20 mins, feed it to Claude, review for 30 mins

same results, 90% less time

the real skill now is knowing what to keep and what to edit
@apollonator3000 Voice notes are undefeated
@apollonator3000 Isn’t there a good set of prompts to “humanize” any text?
@apollonator3000 I do

@apollonator3000 real

I brain dump and ask AI to find studies and reports to back up my claims and it does a fantastic job
@apollonator3000 yup, llms are great at structuring your thoughts. once that’s done, you’ll end up with more copy and even new ideas, which you can then edit to your liking

@apollonator3000 For my ad style, there is so much slight context that AI misses the ball on every time

I hand write 90% of it then use AI to trim the fluff

@apollonator3000 Saves so much time…but you have to know what you want to write and then revise.  One major bugbear I have with all models is they all say “great idea” when I make revisions. I don’t even think all my ideas are great!

@apollonator3000 Been doing this for months. My process: brain dump → AI format → human edit. Tripled my output with better quality.

@apollonator3000 I think a bigger question is does anyone even read long-form copy any more? 

Or do they get AI to summarize it? 

Or do they just read the Google AI summary instead? 

Does anyone even click the links in the AI summary?

@apollonator3000 The first draft is written by me, then I send it to AI to revise it. After tweaking 2-3 times, I get the perfect copy

@apollonator3000 struggling copyrighters cant come to this conclusion 

they will leave the business

@apollonator3000 Couldn't agree more. Strangely enough, I get more joy from chipping away and editing what an LLM has given me from my prompting. Really helps me balance between what I like while also staying on target faster than grind out writing from scratch too!
@apollonator3000 The future isn’t manual it’s strategic feed AI your brain & style and scale like a boss

tweet_text:
SEO isn’t a volume game.

Google wants better answers, not more content.

Quality means: 

→ Covers the topic fully
→ Written for humans
→ Has a point of view
→ Respects the reader’s time.

AI slop fails all of these. Readers bounce. Rankings drop.

Write one thing worth
author: ahmadafterhours
replies:
Agree on quality. Disagree that volume is irrelevant.

One great piece still needs to be found. Topical authority: covering a subject deeply across multiple pages... is how Google decides you're the expert worth ranking.

The real edge isn't one great article. It's a body of work where every piece is worth reading.

Quality sets the floor. Consistency builds the authority.
@ahmadafterhours Write for humans, not bots that’s the real SEO hack.
@ahmadafterhours SEO is also a backlink domain authority game too.
@ahmadafterhours Yep..when content genuinely helps readers, trust builds and traffic follows naturally over time.
@ahmadafterhours Ai format is everywhere! It looks like a sandwich hehe
@ahmadafterhours Yeah we can smell ai slop now!
@ahmadafterhours spot on, ahmad. one killer post > 100 ai dumps every time

@ahmadafterhours You rarely see content worth reading online ATM.

most pages just need to stop wasting that first three paragraphs.
@ahmadafterhours Will keep this in mind for next time👀
@ahmadafterhours Our target audience are humans, not AI bots)
@ahmadafterhours Strongly agree.
@ahmadafterhours Oh so quality over quantity, got it, thanks!

author: perbit3
replies: Companies are throwing $2.5 trillion at AI by 2026, yet most will see zero revenue impact. Why? They're buying tools instead of building strategy. AI only matters when it changes how you make money, not just how fast you work. @elearnindustry Link below.

tweet_text:
ChatGPT can write good copy.

But stop kidding yourself.
YOU can NOT make it write good copy.

Unless…

Unless you can write good copy. Or at the very least know what good copy looks like.

Why?
Because how will you otherwise know when to stop asking it for more versions?
author: GrammarHippy

tweet_text:
How I write 10x better website copy:

Read it out loud.

If it sounds like AI or robotic when you say it, you will feel it.

And that is also what other people will feel.

But if it sounds very natural when you say it, then it will convert.

Because people love human to human
author: b_winzer
replies: @b_winzer The read aloud test is underrated. It forces you to catch all those awkward phrases that look fine written but sound terrible spoken. Natural copy converts.
